Other large cities across the forecast area were also substantially impacted by busted water lines and little to no water pressure. This event also caused 3 fatalities due to hypothermia. One occurred in Hinds County and two in Lauderdale County. The overall monetary losses from this prolonged cold event is a best guess. The economic losses due to downtime for municipalities were not know and not included.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were reports of frozen water valves at many residential homes around the parish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged cold snap, caused by a couple of strong arctic air masses, affected the ArkLaMiss region during the first two weeks of January. This resulted in some record temperatures across the region. Daily highs during that period were in the 20s, 30s and 40s with lows in the teens and 20s. The first ten days of 2010 were the coldest first ten days of any year on record with an average temperature of around 29 degrees, which is around 14 to 16 degrees below normal. This outbreak will not be remembered for any bitterly cold temperatures, it will be remembered for the prolonged period of cold conditions and spans of time below freezing. Due to the duration of the cold, a great deal of damage occurred to the water line infrastructure around the region. The issues from the hard freeze were as follows: water line breaks, and frozen water pipes to homes, especially rural homes; wells freezing up; and water valves being stuck. Due to all the water line breaks, flooding of buildings and homes occurred. By far, the hardest hit area due to broken water lines and overall impact, was the Jackson Metro. The city had to be shut down for 3 to 5 days due to no water pressure. Substantial economic losses occurred due to the downtime of municipalities across the Metro. Other large cities across the forecast area were also substantially impacted by busted water lines and little to no water pressure. This event also caused 3 fatalities due to hypothermia. One occurred in Hinds County and two in Lauderdale County. The overall monetary losses from this prolonged cold event is a best guess. The economic losses due to downtime for municipalities were not know and not included.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water lines were reported broken to a few residential homes in the parish.

An intense storm system moved across the region from February 11, 2010 through February 12, 2010, bringing widespread sleet and snow. Sleet mixed with rain occurred throughout the day on February 11th, before mixing with and changing to snow during the evening hours. A changeover to all snow occurred by midnight on February 12th, with snow continuing well into the morning hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Franklin Police, Fire, and Sheriff Departments along with several reports from the public indicated hail ranging from golf ball to tennis ball size broke numerous windows in the downtown area. This is the largest known hail ever recorded in St. Mary Parish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front that moved through southeast Louisiana during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down about 2 miles north of the Loranger and traveled on an intermittent path approximately 1.5 miles long. 10 to 15 large trees were toppled or uprooted, one barn destroyed, an out building damaged and a house damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved southward across southwest Louisiana on March 20, bringing scattered sleet and snow flurries to portions of central Louisiana from late on March 20 into much of the day on March 21. No accumulation was seen as temperatures remained well above freezing. Strong northerly winds behind the cold front brought unusually low tides to the southwest Louisiana coast for several days, with tides as much as 2 to 3 feet below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freshwater Canal Locks NOS Tide Gauge measured a low tide level of -2.97 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours on March 25 across southwest Louisiana. A few thunderstorms became severe as they raced east-northeastward at up to 70 mph, with numerous reports of large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Survey found severe microburst wind damage along a 2 mile long by 250 yard wide path about 1 mile east of Nuba. A new exit sign on I-49 was blown onto the east service road at the beginning of the path. Twelve large 1 to 3 ton storage buildings, each worth up to $10K, were damaged or destroyed along I-49 at Bay Ridge Road. Debris from the storage buildings was blown up to 100 yards away. Three nearby homes along Bay Ridge Road lost several shingles and a barn received roof damage. Along Highway 743, large tree limbs were blown up to 200 yards away into a pond at a Walmart Distribution Center. Further to the east, a swingset was wrapped around a tree at a home along Highway 743. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down all along the path. Winds were estimated around 90 mph.

These storms propagated northward along a strong outflow boundary and moved into northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana. A very unstable atmosphere awaited this line of storms and during the afternoon, a few reports of downed trees and powerlines were reported across northwest Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerline was downed on Dilg League off of Jewella Avenue in north Shreveport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous, upper level storm system moved across eastern Kansas into southern Missouri during the afternoon and evening hours of May 20th. A portion of this trough swung southward into the middle Red River Valley of northeast Texas, southeast Oklahoma, southwest Arkansas and northern Louisiana. An outflow boundary was present across the region from earlier convection that was present during the morning of the 20th. A moderately unstable airmass, a weak outflow boundary, and plentiful moisture combined with the influence of the upper storm system were all the ingredients needed for strong to severe thunderstorms. The thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts along with large hail across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs and branches were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow affected a large portion of the region, especially locations across central and southern Mississippi, on Thursday night and Friday, February 11th and 12th. The heavy snow was a result of a low pressure system that tracked eastward across the northern Gulf of Mexico, and a vigorous upper level disturbance that moved across the region while a cold air mass was in place. Light precipitation overspread the region late Thursday afternoon into the evening before becoming heavy Thursday night into early Friday morning. The snow tapered off from west to east during the midday hours Friday.||Northern portions of the area, including the Highway 82 corridor, received generally 1 inch or less of snow accumulation. Areas across central and southern parts of the forecast area received the greatest snowfall, with totals ranging mainly between 4 and 6 inches with locally higher amounts. Due to the heavy wet nature of the snow, many large branches and some trees took down power lines across the region. Generally, once 3 inches of snow accumulation was met, damage began to occur. Power outages were common after the event and widespread.||At the National Weather Service office, a total of 4.7 inches of snow fell during the event. This is the 2nd largest February snowfall event and the 10th overall largest snowfall event on record. This heavy snow event was not just a local event. Heavy snow spanned a large portion of the South with a substantial swath of 3 to 6 inches which fell from north-central Texas through north and central Louisiana, central and southern Mississippi, Alabama and Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 6 inches of snow was reported across Tensas Parish, with 6.5 inches reported in Saint Joseph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon and early evening hours of June 28th. These storms developed in a very unstable atmosphere and given the fact that temperatures were well into the 90s and approaching 100 degrees when these thunderstorms developed, there was a sufficient layer of drier air in the lower levels of the atmosphere. Therefore, when these storms dissipated, they produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was blown off a modular home southeast of Lisbon, Louisiana.

Isolated thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon and early evening hours of June 28th. These storms developed in a very unstable atmosphere and given the fact that temperatures were well into the 90s and approaching 100 degrees when these thunderstorms developed, there was a sufficient layer of drier air in the lower levels of the atmosphere. Therefore, when these storms dissipated, they produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in the Olde Oaks subdivision resulting in structural damage to the house.  No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the midday hours of April 24th, an intense supercell thunderstorm produced a violent, long track tornado, which ripped a path across the center of the NWS Jackson, MS forecast area. This tornado touched down just south of I-20, west of Tallulah, in Madison Parish. The tornado intensified quickly and became large as it tracked at a fast pace to the northeast. The tornado moved at roughly 55 mph and was on the ground for 149 miles. The actual time on the ground was 2 hours and 44 minutes. The tornado touched 9 counties along it's path with the most significant damage occurring in Madison Parish, Warren, Yazoo, Holmes, Attala and Choctaw Counties. A great deal of the damage was in the EF2 and EF3 range with a couple locations of EF4 damage in Yazoo and Holmes Counties. Maximum winds peaked around 170 mph. Overall, 10 fatalities occurred along with 146 injuries. Five fatalities occurred in Choctaw County, 4 in Yazoo County and 1 in Holmes County.||This long track tornado ranks as the 4th longest in Mississippi history at 149 miles. It is the widest known tornado with a maximum width of 1.75 miles. It also ranks as the 9th deadliest tornado with 10 fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The start of the long-track violent tornado began around 6 miles west of Tallulah, just to the south of I-20. This tornado did not waste any time becoming strong and quite wide as it downed a few high tension truss towers on the south end of I-20. As the tornado crossed the interstate, a tractor-trailer was flipped over and thrown opposite of the initial traveling direction. The driver of the truck was injured as this occurred. The tornado then traveled quickly northeast and crossed Highway 80 and Willow Bayou Road, just to the northwest of Tallulah. Here, several homes were heavily damaged along with several vehicles and tractors damaged.||The tornado continued to the northeast and crossed Highway 65 and then tracked across the Mississippi River and into far northwest Warren County at Eagle Lake. This tornado destroyed a chemical plant near Highway 65 and the Mississippi River levee, in the community of Omega. A few rail cars were rolled off the track at this location and a large crane was flipped over and destroyed. Several storage tanks were severely damaged as well at this location.||The tornado produced mainly EF3 and EF2 damage across Madison Parish with maximum winds around 155 mph. Timber and power line damage along the path was substantial with dozens of power lines taken down along with thousands of trees snapped and uprooted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 2.5 inches of rain between 8 am and 10 am at City Hall. Numerous roads were reported to be impassible. Cars were floating in several intersections.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in western Caddo Parish in the community of Greenwood.  The homeowners were in the house when the lightning strike occurred.  There were no injuries but the lightning resulted in a large house fire and the house was deemed a total loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very dry conditions continued across the parishes of northeast Louisiana through the month of July. While several of the parishes were already experiencing extreme drought (D3) when July started, the D3 area actually expanded more as July moved along. Normal rainfall across this area for July is 3 to 4 inches. Only 1 to 2 inches actually fell during the month. Crops continued to be put under stress from the dry and hot conditions. The likely impact will be less crop yields when harvest arrives in early Fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was 1 to 3 inches below normal for the month with the parish seeing severe (D2) drought conditions.

These storms continued into northwest Louisiana during the early morning hours of April 24th.

The tornado touched down at around 6:57 AM CDT, about 7 miles west of Marion along Game Reserve Road, knocking down a few trees along the road and in the forest nearby. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow affected a large portion of the region, especially locations across central and southern Mississippi, on Thursday night and Friday, February 11th and 12th. The heavy snow was a result of a low pressure system that tracked eastward across the northern Gulf of Mexico, and a vigorous upper level disturbance that moved across the region while a cold air mass was in place. Light precipitation overspread the region late Thursday afternoon into the evening before becoming heavy Thursday night into early Friday morning. The snow tapered off from west to east during the midday hours Friday.||Northern portions of the area, including the Highway 82 corridor, received generally 1 inch or less of snow accumulation. Areas across central and southern parts of the forecast area received the greatest snowfall, with totals ranging mainly between 4 and 6 inches with locally higher amounts. Due to the heavy wet nature of the snow, many large branches and some trees took down power lines across the region. Generally, once 3 inches of snow accumulation was met, damage began to occur. Power outages were common after the event and widespread.||At the National Weather Service office, a total of 4.7 inches of snow fell during the event. This is the 2nd largest February snowfall event and the 10th overall largest snowfall event on record. This heavy snow event was not just a local event. Heavy snow spanned a large portion of the South with a substantial swath of 3 to 6 inches which fell from north-central Texas through north and central Louisiana, central and southern Mississippi, Alabama and Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 3 inches of snow fell in East Carroll Parish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours on March 25 across southwest Louisiana. A few thunderstorms became severe as they raced east-northeastward at up to 70 mph, with numerous reports of large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KATC-TV reported quarter size hail at Ira Street and Cankton Road in Carencro.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Survey found microburst wind damage over a 3 mile long by 1/4 mile wide path along Gauthier Road east of Lake Charles Regional Airport. One mobile home had major roof damage in the Quail Ridge Mobile Home Park. A church lost siding and several mobile homes received minor roof and skirting damage around the intersection of Tom Hebert Road and Gauthier Road. A large sign was blown down at Corbina Road. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down along the path. Winds were estimated around 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms moved northeast over portions of Northeast Louisiana. In East Carroll Parish, one storm produced a lightning strike which hit a house. This strike resulted in a fire and the lost of the structure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike was reported and caused a house fire in Transylvania. This resulted in the total loss of the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent weather disturbance brought showers and thunderstorms to the region. Some of these storms produced hail up to the size of quarters.

A potent upper disturbance brought severe weather to portions of northeast Louisiana on the morning of the 24th. These storms produced walnut sized hail in Morehouse Parish. This was also the day where an intense supercell thunderstorm produced the long track EF4 tornado, which started just west of Tallulah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Walnut sized hail was reported just northwest of Beekman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed across southwest Louisiana during the afternoon hours on May 26. Over a dozen reports of large hail, damaging winds, and even one tornado were received. Damage totals reached into the millions of dollars, making this the worst severe thunderstorm event for the Spring 2010 season in the WFO Lake Charles county warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported at Hornbeck High School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed across southwest Louisiana during the afternoon hours on May 30. Wind damage, large hail, and flash flooding were all reported around the Lake Charles area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey found downburst wind damage in a half mile wide by 3 mile long swath across south Lake Charles from the McNeese State University campus southwestward to around the Nelson Road and Ham Reid Road intersection. The worst damage was in and near the McNeese campus, where several trees and power poles were blown down. A few signs at nearby businesses were also damaged, and a flag pole was knocked down along Common Street. Further to the southwest, trees and power lines were blown down and several homes received minor roof damage. Pea to marble size hail was also reported in several locations along the path of the downburst. All damage was in a south to southwest direction, with winds estimated around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed across southwest Louisiana during the afternoon hours on May 30. Wind damage, large hail, and flash flooding were all reported around the Lake Charles area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KPLC-TV and Calcasieu Parish Sheriff office reported widespread street flooding across south Lake Charles, with most entrance and exit ramps along Interstate 210 closed due to high water. Some automobiles stalled due to high water in the area from around I-210 to the McNeese State University campus, and at least one home on Ernest Street and another home on Common Street were flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey found a small but severe microburst struck Westlake High School and adjacent areas in the central part of Westlake. This 200-yard wide by 300-yard long microburst occurred within a larger scale downburst that affected much of the city of Westlake. At the high school, the western one-third of the aluminum metal gymnasium roof was ripped off and blown westward into the adjacent parking lot, overturning one vehicle and damaging three others. A basketball team inside the gymnasium was not injured. The press box on the western side of the football stadium was destroyed, with debris left dangling between nearby light poles and the stands. Across the street, the Westlake Fire Department building received damage to the east side of the building. Because of the damage, all classes at Westlake High School were canceled on May 27, which was to be the last day of school. All damage was blown in a west-southwest direction, with winds estimated around 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong upper level low slowly moved across southeast Texas and southwest Louisiana from June 3 into June 4. This system produced numerous showers and thunderstorms, which spawned a couple of tornadoes, wind damage, and coastal flooding across southwest Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: St. Mary Parish Sheriff Deputy reported a brief tornado touchdown with no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the heating of the day across Southwest Arkansas into Northwest Louisiana.  This activity developed ahead of a frontal boundary that was pulled up nearly stationary across central Arkansas.  Afternoon temperatures were between 100 and 105 degrees so instability was excessive across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed near Hwy. 167.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of high temperatures in the upper 90s and dewpoints in the low 70s resulted in high heat index values ranging from 105 to 110 degrees across central Louisiana during the afternoon hours on June 21. A two year old boy died of heat exhaustion in Rapides Parish when he climbed inside a vehicle and became trapped.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beauregard Daily News reported that a two-year old boy died of heat exhaustion in Hineston when he climbed into an unlocked car and became trapped between 3 PM and 4 PM. He was reportedly trapped for less than one hour. At nearby Alexandria International Airport, the high temperature was 99 degrees with a dewpoint of 72 degrees. This resulted in a heat index value of 110 degrees around the time of the fatality.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system affected the region and helped to trigger some severe storms. The storms produced a mix of hail and thunderstorm wind damage across a portion of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and utility lines were blown down along Ray Mars Road southwest of Harrisonburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system affected the region and helped to trigger some severe storms. The storms produced a mix of hail and thunderstorm wind damage across a portion of the area.
A strong upper disturbance, combined with an unstable and moist air mass, to produce a few severe storms across portions of northeast Louisiana.

Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms affected areas near the coast, with one report of wind damage in Vermilion Parish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vermilion Parish Sheriff Office reported trees and power lines blown down along Highway 35 about 2 miles south of Kaplan, with power outages in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees down in various locations around Slidell. Reported by Slidell Police Department.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong to severe thunderstorms developed across northwest and north central Louisiana during the afternoon and early evening hours of August 16th. These storms developed behind a nearly stationary frontal boundary and were moving from northeast to southwest across the northern half of the state. A weak shear axis existed across the state at that time and the storms developed in a moderately unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed into powerlines on Ellerbe Road near Flournoy Lucas Road in southeast Shreveport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms produced large hail and one lightning fatality.

The child was standing near a tree when the tree was struck by lightning from a thunderstorm. No rain was occurring at the time of the lightning strike. The boy was admitted to the hospital, and died on June 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a strong area of high pressure. There was enough instability near the surface with daytime heating to produce a strong dust devil in Franklin Parish in Winnsboro. It blew the metal roof and insulation off of a small storage building and damaged some nearby trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist airmass over the region, coupled with a weak upper low to the east of the forecast area, triggered a few severe thunderstorms both afternoons. The storms primarily generated gusty winds although some hail up to 1 inch in diameter was found.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced several reports of severe weather during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service team has surveyed the damage and concluded that it was caused by a microburst. Wind speeds were estimated at 70 to 80 mph. Widespread small limb damage was noted as well as a few medium to large size trees downed. Damage was confined to an area bounded by Patricia St. to the north...Alexander St. to the west...Rowley St. to the east and St. Bernard Highway to the south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced several reports of severe weather during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were downed on Omega Drive. Report was relayed by emergency manager. Time of report based on radar and surface observations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported via the KPLC-TV Viewernet website that a large tree fell and crushed a car on Holbrook Road south of Dequincy, which was confirmed by photos. Hail of unknown size was also reported.

Damage totals reached into the millions of dollars, making this the worst severe thunderstorm event for the Spring 2010 season in the WFO Lake Charles county warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KATC-TV reported dime to quarter size hail in Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms across south central Louisiana caused a lightning strike that set a house on fire in Iberia Parish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily Iberian reported lightning struck a home at 511 Lucerne Road near New Iberia and set it on fire. Photos indicated the home was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small tropical low moved inland along the south central Louisiana coast from late in the evening hours on July 5 into the early morning hours of July 6, then continued northwest across southwest Louisiana into east Texas from July 7 into July 8. This system produced heavy rainfall and flash flooding across south central Louisiana, and minor coastal flooding along the southern Louisiana coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iberia Parish Emergency Management and media reports indicate widespread flooding occurred across central and southern parts of the parish. At least 21 homes were reportedly flooded with the majority of flooded homes in Grand Marais and New Iberia. The First Baptist Church in Lydia was also flooded, along with an unknown number of businesses and other buildings. A total of 18 roads were closed due to flooding, with over a foot of water covering some roadways. Numerous cars stalled out in flood waters, and some high water rescues were performed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorms associated with the remnant circulation of Tropical Depression 5 produced produced heavy rain across Metro New Orleans and some flooding in the Metairie area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water intruded into the first floor of an apartment comlex in southeast Metairie near Ochsner Medical Center. There were also several flooded streets in Bridge City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms associated with the remnant circulation of Tropical Depression Five produced heavy rainfall. Several reports of flash flooding in southeast Louisiana occurred during during the afternoon hours. This was the second passage of the remnant circulation of Tropical Depression 5 across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Louisiana Highway 10 was flooded near Morganza.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms associated with the remnant circulation of Tropical Depression Five produced heavy rainfall. Several reports of flash flooding in southeast Louisiana occurred during during the afternoon hours. This was the second passage of the remnant circulation of Tropical Depression 5 across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain resulting in minor flooding in the Baton Rouge area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain flooded Wooddale Avenue with flood water entering a food market.

Across the NWS Jackson, MS forecast area, 19 record highs were set between August 1st and 4th. Additionally, relatively high humidity levels made conditions even more oppressive, with heat index readings surpassing 110 degrees in many areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds from a thunderstorm blew down six trees on Buford Creel Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of rain caused wash outs on  several roads and near bridges in northern Washington Parish. More than 20 roads and bridges were damaged in the parish.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jeanerette LSU Ag Center measured 7.93 inches of rainfall from midnight through 2 PM CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours of August 9th. Some of the thunderstorms brought heavy rain and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of Tropical Storm Bonnie moved across the region and produced heavy rainfall in a several locations resulting in flash flooding in portions of Southeast Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, possibly as much as 8 inches in 90 minutes, produced flooding of streets and approximately 110 homes in the parish. The water in homes was generally several inches in depth. Media reports indicated around 20 homes were affected in the Lynndale subdivision and 15 homes in Brusly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small tropical low moved inland along the south central Louisiana coast from late in the evening hours on July 5 into the early morning hours of July 6, then continued northwest across southwest Louisiana into east Texas from July 7 into July 8. This system produced heavy rainfall and flash flooding across south central Louisiana, and minor coastal flooding along the southern Louisiana coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Calcasieu Pass NOS Tide Gauge measured a peak high tide level of 3.75 feet at 730 AM CDT on July 7, and another peak high tide level of 3.67 feet at 130 AM CDT on July 8. Tide levels were above 3 feet MLLW for two extended periods of time, from 1042 PM CDT on July 6 through 3 PM CDT on July 7, and again from 1048 PM CDT on July 7 through 224 PM CDT on July 8. These high tides caused considerable coastal flooding in Cameron Parish, and the Cameron Pilot newspaper reported several roads in Cameron were underwater at the times of the peak high tides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms associated with Tropical Depression Five produced flooding in Pointe Coupee and East Baton Rouge Parishes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of street flooding were received in Baton Rouge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms associated with Tropical Depression Five produced flooding in Pointe Coupee and East Baton Rouge Parishes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that a house was taking on water on Napoleon Street in New Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms associated with Tropical Depression Five produced flooding in Pointe Coupee and East Baton Rouge Parishes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pointe Coupee Law Enforcement reported ongoing street flooding and water in homes along several local streets, including New Roads Street...Louisiana Street...Richey Street...Janis Street and Berthier Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced isolated flooding south and west of New Orleans.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flooding in central and southern portions of Lafourche Parish. Several roads were closed by the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced isolated flooding south and west of New Orleans.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of rain produced flooding of five homes in Des Allemands, Bayou Gauche, Boutte and Paradis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms triggered minor flooding in several areas of Tangipahoa Parish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced street flooding in several areas of Hammond and surrounding portions of Tangipahoa Parish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over five inches of rain from the remnants of Tropical Depression Five produced flooding over portions of southeast Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of rain from the remnants of Tropical Depression Five flooded yards and approached homes near Hammond High School east of Hammond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A four day stretch of extreme temperatures occurred across the region to start off the month of August. High pressure was firmly entrenched across the southeast and allowed temperatures to soar into the triple digits across much of the region. Across the NWS Jackson, MS forecast area, 19 record highs were set between August 1st and 4th. Additionally, relatively high humidity levels made conditions even more oppressive, with heat index readings surpassing 110 degrees in many areas.
The combination of a weak front, strong upper disturbance, and daytime heating, increased instability and brought a round of severe storms to the region on the afternoon and evening of August 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and limbs were blown down in the Pioneer Community and surrounding area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions continued across northeast portions of Louisiana during September. The dry stretch resulted in a combination of severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought conditions continuing across northeast Louisiana. Crops were put under more stress from the hot and dry conditions. The likely impact will be less crop yields when harvest arrives in early Fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was 3 to 4 inches below normal for much of the parish, with extreme drought conditions persisting for most of the parish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnant circulation of Tropical Depression 5 moved northwest into Avoyelles Parish and stalled during the early morning hours on August 18. Nocturnal convection exploded in coverage near the circulation center, and continued throughout much of the day, resulting in widespread flash flooding across Avoyelles Parish. Radar estimated as much as 15 inches of rain fell on August 18 between midnight and 4 PM CDT across western and central portions of the parish. Estimates from local media and the Red Cross indicate a total of 25 homes and 15 businesses were flooded, mainly in the Mansura, Hessmer, Marksville, and Bunkie areas.||In addition to the flooding, lightning struck and killed three heads of cattle on August 18 and destroyed a house on August 19.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rainfall total of 9.55 inches was measured at the Bunkie Water Plant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions continued across northeast portions of Louisiana during September. The dry stretch resulted in a combination of severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought conditions continuing across northeast Louisiana. Crops were put under more stress from the hot and dry conditions. The likely impact will be less crop yields when harvest arrives in early Fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was 1 to 2 inches below normal for much of the parish, with severe to extreme drought conditions across the entire parish for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across central Louisiana during the afternoon hours on September 19. One small thunderstorm produced a microburst that caused damage in Pineville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alexandria Town Talk reported a microburst struck portions of southern Pineville near the Red River. A tree was blown onto a house on Harrison Street, with other trees falling onto roadways at the intersection of Sanders Street and Lee Street. A large tree fell onto a mobile home and an adjacent car on Grant Street. A wooden Zion Hill Baptist Church sign was blown to the southeast into the median of the Cottingham Expressway. Several power lines were also blown down with up to 50 homes losing power. The microburst damage path was approximately 0.3 miles long and 50 yards wide. All damage was in a general northwest to southeast direction with wind speeds estimated around 65 mph.

High pressure was firmly entrenched across the southeast and allowed temperatures to soar into the triple digits across much of the region. Across the NWS Jackson, MS forecast area, 19 record highs were set between August 1st and 4th. Additionally, relatively high humidity levels made conditions even more oppressive, with heat index readings surpassing 110 degrees in many areas.
Very dry conditions for the last several months across northern Louisiana resulted in a very high fire danger across many parishes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire, believed to have been started by a lightning strike, began burning during the evening hours of October 11th across the Kisatchie National Forest approximately 20 miles southeast of Natchitoches, Louisiana in southern Natchitoches Parish.  This fire quickly spread to envelop approximately 4,363 acres of timberland with over 70 personnel called in to fight the wildfire.  There were no structures damaged or destroyed by the wildfire because of its location in the Kisatchie National Forest.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire quickly spread out of control just south of Winnfield, Louisiana on October 28th.  Bull dozers were used to push the fire lines that were continually jumped by the fire.  The blaze moved from the ground to the crown of some 30-foot tall trees and quickly spread.  Tanker drops were also used to try and contain the fire and protect homes.  The blaze was contained that evening but not before 250 acres were burned with the loss of some out buildings.  Firefighters on scene estimated that 10 homes were in imminent danger but were saved by firefighting efforts.  The fire was located near Cedar Drive and Old Alexandria Road.  In addition to the out buildings, one rent house was destroyed.  One fireman suffered a minor injury as well.

Very dry conditions continued across northeast portions of Louisiana during August. The dry stretch resulted in a combination of severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought conditions continuing across northeast Louisiana despite a few locations receiving above normal rainfall for the month. Crops were put under more stress from the hot and dry conditions. The likely impact will be less crop yields when harvest arrives in early Fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was near to slightly above normal for the month with the parish seeing some improvement to Moderate (D1) drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent weather system impacted the region on October 12th and brought a round of severe storms to the region. This system combined with increasing moisture and cold temperatures aloft to generate a band of scattered thunderstorms. These storms produced a mix of hail and damaging wind gusts. Most of the hail was quarter size with the largest stone, golf ball size, which occurred in northeast Louisiana.

The remnant circulation of Tropical Depression 5 moved northwest into Avoyelles Parish and stalled during the early morning hours on August 18. Nocturnal convection exploded in coverage near the circulation center, and continued throughout much of the day, resulting in widespread flash flooding across Avoyelles Parish. Radar estimated as much as 15 inches of rain fell on August 18 between midnight and 4 PM CDT across western and central portions of the parish. Estimates from local media and the Red Cross indicate a total of 25 homes and 15 businesses were flooded, mainly in the Mansura, Hessmer, Marksville, and Bunkie areas.||In addition to the flooding, lightning struck and killed three heads of cattle on August 18 and destroyed a house on August 19.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marksville Weekly News reported a house at 1556 Highway 1192 was struck by lightning and destroyed. Three fire departments responded to fight the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent weather system, for late October, impacted our region and brought a round of showers and thunderstorms to the forecast area on October 24th and 25th. This system produced severe weather across a good portion of the South with nearly 300 severe weather reports stretching from Texas to northern Georgia. Across the NWS Jackson forecast area, a number of these storms reached severe limits for locations north of Interstate 20 and primarily produced damaging winds. A few additional instances of hail and flash flooding also occurred.

An area of low pressure was exiting the area on the morning of October 20th. Sufficient low level moisture was in place overnight to allow a couple severe thunderstorms to develop across northwest Louisiana in the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered trees were downed across the area with some power outages reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong upper level low slowly moved across southeast Texas and southwest Louisiana from June 3 into June 4. This system produced numerous showers and thunderstorms, which spawned a couple of tornadoes, wind damage, and coastal flooding across southwest Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: St. Mary Parish Sheriff Deputy reported a tornado touched down near the intersection of Highway 90 and Highway 318 southeast of Jeanerette. A tree reportedly fell on an abandoned mobile home. No other damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid weather produced high temperatures near 100 degrees and heat index values near 110 degrees in portions of southeast Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 40 year old man died due to heat stroke while fishing in Delacroix. Heat index values were near 110 degrees in the area at the time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pointe Coupee Law Enforcement reported a home on fire on Cooks Landing Drive in Ventress due to lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused damage to a home and knocked a tree onto an automobile and a home in East Baton Rouge Parish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused electrical damage to a home, and knocked down a tree which fell onto an automobile and a home in Baker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced two reports of severe weather in the New Orleans metro area.

Northeast Louisiana was quite dry in recent months and has run 2-3 inches below normal on precipitation since March. Most areas have only received around 2 inches of rain each month, although some areas have struggled to get as much as 1 inch. Due to the lack of substantial rainfall, extreme drought conditions began in late June across the parishes. The drought conditions continued into the month of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts have been running 1 to 3 inches below normal since earlier this year.

An NWS Storm Survey determined damage was likely from a microburst that spread from southeast to northwest across the farm. A few trees were also blown down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very dry conditions continued across the parishes of northeast Louisiana through the month of July. While several of the parishes were already experiencing extreme drought (D3) when July started, the D3 area actually expanded more as July moved along. Normal rainfall across this area for July is 3 to 4 inches. Only 1 to 2 inches actually fell during the month. Crops continued to be put under stress from the dry and hot conditions. The likely impact will be less crop yields when harvest arrives in early Fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was 1 to 3 inches below normal for the month with the parish seeing severe (D2) drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell thunderstorm developed in the afternoon hours near Lake Charles, and then moved northeast towards the Ville Platte area before continuing into southern Mississippi. Several reports of damaging winds, large hail, and one tornado were received along its path across Evangeline, northern St. Landry, and southern Avoyelles Parishes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chatman's Grocery Store reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a weak disturbance aloft which moved across southern Arkansas into north central and northeast Louisiana.  The atmosphere was moderately unstable and as a result...with the heating of the day...widely scattered showers and thunderstorms developed...mainly across northeast Louisiana. One of these storms produced large hail during the evening of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell in the community of Sterlington, Louisiana and the hail cracked a windshield in 5 different places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms associated with a cold front produced isolated reports of severe weather over Southeast Louisiana during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported in Burns and Southwood communities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Additional showers and thunderstorms developed across southwest Louisiana during the afternoon hours on May 17. Some of these storms caused wind damage in and near the Lake Charles area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee estimated 60 mph winds in downtown Lake Charles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Depression Five stalled across Avoyelles Parish on August 18, causing nearly sixteen hours of heavy rainfall. The resulting rainfall totals of up to 15 inches resulted in widespread flash flooding throughout the parish, with a total of 25 homes and 15 business reportedly flooded. ||Homes seeing flooding included apartments on Tony Roy Road in Mansura, several homes along Highway 114 between Mansura and Hessmer, and a duplex in Simmesport. A woman had to be rescued from her flooded home on Large Road in Mansura. Businesses receiving flooding included KLIL radio station in Moreauville, Poret Drug Store in Cottonport, Bunkie Record Newspaper in Bunkie, and all 7 businesses on Main Street in Bunkie.||Roadway closures included Highway 114, Leglise Street, Tony Road, Large Road, Merrick Street, and St. Jean Street in Mansura; and Highway 107 in Plaucheville. Six to seven vehicles stalled in high water along Highway 114 between Mansura and Hessmer, and other vehicles reportedly became stranded in flood waters on Large Road and Schexnyder Road in Mansura, and Little River Road in Spring Bayou.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak unstable airmass developed across northern Louisiana ahead of a cold front which by the afternoon hours had moved across southern Arkansas into northeast Texas.  This frontal boundary was accompanied by a weak upper level trough moving across the Southern Plains.  During the late afternoon into the early evening hours...thunderstorms developed ahead of the front across northern Louisiana. A few of these thunderstorms became severe producing hail and strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs were blown down and power outages were reported along Hwy. 133 between Columbia and Hebert, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm produced localized flash flooding in the New Orleans metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two businesses were flooded near Jefferson Highway and Metairie Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong upper level low slowly moved across southeast Texas and southwest Louisiana from June 3 into June 4. This system produced numerous showers and thunderstorms, which spawned a couple of tornadoes, wind damage, and coastal flooding across southwest Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Franklin Police Department reported most streets in town were flooded, with Iberia Street and Weber Street closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours on May 25 across south central Louisiana. One storm in St. Mary Parish produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KATC-TV reported penny to quarter size hail in Verdunville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms began developing across the region during the late evening hours of May 16th and continued into the predawn hours of May 17th.  The storms encountered a moderately unstable airmass south of the Interstate 20 corridor of northern Louisiana.  A strong outflow boundary served as the focus for the storms.  The storms produced damaging wind gusts along with large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed near the community of Thompson Ferry, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced minor damage over southeast Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed by thunderstorm winds on Highway 444 between Highway 63 and Bull Run Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced several severe weather reports during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer at KNBG...NAS New Orleans...reported quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist airmass over the region, coupled with a weak upper low to the east of the forecast area, triggered a few severe thunderstorms both afternoons. The storms primarily generated gusty winds although some hail up to 1 inch in diameter was found.

Several strong to severe thunderstorms developed across northwest and north central Louisiana during the afternoon and early evening hours of August 16th. These storms developed behind a nearly stationary frontal boundary and were moving from northeast to southwest across the northern half of the state. A weak shear axis existed across the state at that time and the storms developed in a moderately unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on New Natchitoches Road in West Monroe.
